bullets: 15 large language models from seven vendors evaluated on a 258-node vintage-correct macroeconomic panel of time-indexed financial decision tasks.

HindsightBench, a black-box audit chaining a four-arm date-manipulation matrix and dual memory probes, profiles how models leak realized-outcome knowledge into decisions; tested models include Qwen and other open and proprietary systems.
The date-trigger reflex tracks training generation rather than scale, and behaviorally effective knowledge cutoffs span 22 months and precede vendor-reported dates by up to eight months.

Single production deployment in a data-intensive enterprise; a conversational business-intelligence system answering natural-language questions over a governed data warehouse of financial and operational KPIs.

Unnamed large language models generate SQL constrained by a certified metrics catalog, schema-linking retrieval, a two-pass generation-critique step, and a read-only sandbox; no accuracy benchmark is reported.
The authors report large reductions in time-to-answer for recurring questions and reduced dependence on data-analyst intermediation, with validation limited to a single deployment.
Original 2026 survey of 50 employees at a single two-year public college, an 18.9 percent response rate, studying unauthorized generative-AI use in higher education.
No model is used by the researchers; the paper documents employee use of ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude and Copilot and proposes a seven-dimension shadow-AI governance-gap framework.
98 percent reported basic AI literacy and 84 percent wanted training, yet most used unsanctioned consumer tools such as ChatGPT at 72 percent, indicating institutional enablement gaps.
Consumer preference elicitation for products and features; multiple large language models queried dozens of times per survey question, with estimates compared to human willingness-to-pay studies.
Unnamed LLMs generate distributions of survey responses to estimate willingness-to-pay, and fine-tuning on prior human survey data is tested; outputs are checked against human study estimates.
LLM willingness-to-pay is sometimes comparable to human estimates but often inaccurate or wrong-signed; fine-tuning improves alignment only within the same product category and population.
validation_note: LLM willingness-to-pay compared to human survey estimates
